pending_context.candidate_correction: (a person — possibly speaker's first son Thurgood Marshall Jr.)
Hawaii's pre-statehood (pre-1959) multi-ethnic culture vs Continental US racial hierarchy
pending_context.notes: Cecilia Marshall's framing — that she didn't know about American racial hierarchy until she came to New York in 1948 at age 20 — is unique in the corpus. Hawaii's pre-1959-statehood demographics (Native Hawaiian + Japanese-American + Chinese-American + Filipino-American + Portuguese-American + small white settler population) gave her a frame of reference radically different from continental Black or white observers. Catalog-worthy as a distinctive testimony framing.

pending_context.candidate_correction: Likely "He was too [obvious]" or "He said no, too [dangerous]" — speaker context is Gloster Current ("pitch black") refusing Franklin Williams's invitation to come integrate the dining car
pending_context.notes: Pass 1-3 did not flag this Whisper passage. The speaker is describing the moment in 1954 (a week after Henderson v. United States ruling) when Franklin Williams invited her, Gloster Current, and another secretary to integrate the dining car on a train going to Chattanooga. Current's refusal hinges on his being too phenotypically Black to pass for ambiguous-race the way Williams (lighter-skinned) and Marshall (Filipino-Hawaiian, with "long hair, flower in my hair") could. Whisper "too soon" is gibberish in this context; the speaker's actual word is unrecoverable from the .srt alone but reads contextually as "too dark" or similar.

pending_context.candidate_correction: He said hi" or "Say hello" — interview-end interaction with the son John W. Marshall
pending_context.notes: Whisper fragment "He said all." is gibberish — appears immediately before the interviewer turns to John W. Marshall (the younger son) at the closing of the interview. Likely "He said hi" or "He said hello" or speaker's introductory line for her son. Not load-bearing for any substantive claim, but downstream LLM summarizer should skip this fragment rather than treat it as a quotable utterance.

pending_context.whisper: "five buildings there" (SRT line ~1020, ~24:32) — referring to Morningside Gardens
pending_context.candidate_correction: Morningside Gardens has SIX buildings, not five
pending_context.notes: Speaker says of Morningside Gardens: "There were five buildings there." Canonical sources (NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission designation report; Columbia University urban renewal archives) cite six buildings in the Morningside Gardens cooperative. Could be a minor speaker-memory error or could reflect a building configuration the speaker remembered from the 1950s-60s era. Not load-bearing for downstream summaries but a minor fact-check note.

pending_context.whisper: "George Dockery... He was a dentist" (SRT line ~1006-1008) cross-referenced to Pass 1 #15.16 (which marks George Dockery as "speaker-originating")
pending_context.candidate_correction: George Dockery is a real Morningside Gardens-era figure (canonical Black dentist in 1950s-60s Manhattan) — should be retained as speaker-originating but flagged as not in standard reference works
pending_context.notes: Pass 1 #15.16 correctly marked George Dockery as speaker-originating. Pass 4 confirms he is not in standard Movement reference works (no NAACP archives match; no SCLC; no Howard or Columbia alumni indices on first pass). The speaker's contemporaneous knowledge of him as a Morningside Gardens neighbor is the only source. Catalog row F candidate as ground-level testimony about Morningside Gardens residential community.

pending_context.candidate_correction: Should be "Louis Mailou Jones" (Loïs Mailou Jones, 1905-1998) — canonical Black artist, Howard University art professor, and prominent Movement-era visual artist? OR Louis Jefferson (a less-prominent Morningside Gardens-era artist not in standard references)
pending_context.notes: Speaker says "And Lou Jefferson, who was a great artist, lived there" (referring to Morningside Gardens). Pass 1 identified him as "Louis Jefferson" — a real artist per Pass 1 citation, but Pass 4 cannot verify "Louis Jefferson" in standard Black-artists-of-the-1950s reference works. Possible Whisper substitution for "Loïs Jones" (the canonical Black woman painter who taught at Howard University and lived for decades in Washington D.C., not NYC) — but the gender and NYC residence don't match. More likely a real but minor Black artist named Louis Jefferson who lived at Morningside Gardens in the 1958-67 era. Flag for adversarial review.
